coredata.dc:description: Rational design of Ni-based electrocatalysts for methanol electrooxidation reaction (MOR) and in-depth understanding of their catalytic mechanism remain a challenge. Herein, carbon dots/graphene heterostructure is constructed to load Ni(OH)2 and the role of carbon dots focuses on regulating distribution and electronic property of Ni(OH)2. Regulation mechanism of carbon dots on Ni(OH)2 for optimizing MOR activity are revealed by in-situ Raman, in-situ FTIR, operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy, and so on. It’s found carbon dots could disperse Ni species and regulate their distribution configuration. Appropriate amount of carbon dots promotes the formation of highly dispersed CDs&Ni(OH)2 type. Because of strong interaction between Ni(OH)2 and CDs, coordination environment and local electronic structure of Ni in Ni-CDs/G can be regulated by carbon dots, leading to phase-ratio variation of α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2 and d-band center shift of Ni(OH)2. For the best Ni-4CDs/G electrocatalyst, more formed α-Ni(OH)2 lead to d-band center downshift from the EF level and optimized its adsorption of methanol. Ni-4CDs/G shows the specific activity of 299.2 mA cm−2 and mass activity of 3756.5 mA mg−1 at peak current. In water splitting test, lower initial overpotential of 140 mV is found in methanol electrolysis than that in water electrolysis. This opens a new door to regulate the metal-based electrocatalysts by CDs and will inspire more people to explore CDs-based materials for electrocatalytic application.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Revealing the regulation mechanism of carbon dots on Ni(OH)2 for optimizing methanol electrooxidation activity

Rational design of Ni-based electrocatalysts for methanol electrooxidation reaction (MOR) and in-depth understanding of their catalytic mechanism remain a challenge. Herein, carbon dots/graphene heterostructure is constructed to load Ni(OH)2 and the role of carbon dots focuses on regulating distribution and electronic property of Ni(OH)2. Regulation mechanism of carbon dots on Ni(OH)2 for optimizing MOR activity are revealed by in-situ Raman, in-situ FTIR, operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy, and so on. It’s found carbon dots could disperse Ni species and regulate their distribution configuration. Appropriate amount of carbon dots promotes the formation of highly dispersed CDs&Ni(OH)2 type. Because of strong interaction between Ni(OH)2 and CDs, coordination environment and local electronic structure of Ni in Ni-CDs/G can be regulated by carbon dots, leading to phase-ratio variation of α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2 and d-band center shift of Ni(OH)2. For the best Ni-4CDs/G electrocatalyst, more formed α-Ni(OH)2 lead to d-band center downshift from the EF level and optimized its adsorption of methanol. Ni-4CDs/G shows the specific activity of 299.2 mA cm−2 and mass activity of 3756.5 mA mg−1 at peak current. In water splitting test, lower initial overpotential of 140 mV is found in methanol electrolysis than that in water electrolysis. This opens a new door to regulate the metal-based electrocatalysts by CDs and will inspire more people to explore CDs-based materials for electrocatalytic application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methanol electrooxidation reaction (MOR) is a vital half-reaction in direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) and other electrochemical devices [1–3]. Despite of that, MOR is also expected to be a promising reaction to substitute oxygen evolution reaction (OER) for hydrogen production. This new coupled cell could not only reduce the energy consumption to produce hydrogen, but also generate high value-added chemicals such as formic acid simultaneously. Nevertheless, the sluggish kinetics of MOR caused by multi-electron transfer is still an industrial bottleneck [4]. The currently efficient MOR electrocatalysts are generally expensive Pt group metals (PGMs), and the high price limits their wide applications. In addition, the active sites on PGMs could be poisoned by the strong adsorption of CO intermediate, causing rapid decay of current and stability for MOR [5,6].

Recently, Ni-based non-precious materials have been applied as MOR electrocatalysts [4,7–12]. To promote the intrinsic activities, different Ni-based electrocatalysts such as metal aerogels, bimetallic alloy, oxides, hydroxides, and chalcogenides, have been synthesized. Moreover, to improve the whole conductivity of Ni-based materials, they are often coupled with carbon materials, such as graphene. Previous studies have also reported that NiOOH is considered a critical active species resulted from Ni(OH)2 via Ni(II)/Ni(III) transformation [8]. Generally, there are two types of crystalline phases for NiOOH: β phase and γ phase. γ‑NiOOH is evolved from α-Ni(OH)2 while β‑NiOOH is generated from β‑Ni(OH)2. Therefore the crystalline phase of NiOOH is decided by the crystalline phase of Ni(OH)2. Both α-Ni(OH)2 and β‑Ni(OH)2 can be regarded as the layered accumulation of NiO2. The difference of α-Ni(OH)2 and β‑Ni(OH)2 is that H2O, metal ions and other anions are inserted into the α-Ni(OH)2 layers while no H2O and other particles are inserted into the β-Ni(OH)2 layers [7,12–16]. This leads to the greater spacing between α-Ni(OH)2 layers than that between β-Ni(OH)2 layers, and α-Ni(OH)2 is more defective than β-Ni(OH)2. It’s believed that the β‑NiOOH shows super MOR activity to γ‑NiOOH due to specific local environment of Ni-O. But the defective α-Ni(OH)2 with high electric conductivity is beneficial for reactant adsorption and electron transfer. Therefore, the synergistic effect of α-Ni(OH)2 and β‑Ni(OH)2 would promote the overall MOR performance of NiOOH.

To promote the MOR activity of NiOOH, the ratio between α-Ni(OH)2 and β‑Ni(OH)2 also needs to be regulated. But related research about regulation on phase ratio of Ni(OH)2 is rare. Carbon dots (CDs) have abundant functional groups on the surface and high specific surface area, These excellent properties of CDs make them a promising material for electrocatalysis [17–22]. Moreover, the abundant surface functional groups on CDs could interact with metal species. Their strong interaction plays an important role in determining the electronic state and local charge distribution of metal species [20–23]. Thus, it is suspected that the carbon dots would have the ability to regulate the distribution and the electronic structure of metal electrocatalysts.

In view of this, here we construct the efficient functional material of Ni-carbon dots/graphene (Ni-CDs/G) for MOR. For the first time, CDs are applied to promote intrinsic activity of Ni based catalysts. The optimized phase ratio of α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2 for enhanced MOR performance is regulated by the strong interaction between CDs and Ni(OH)2. What’s more, CDs also could regulate the distribution of Ni(OH)2 to improve its dispersion and distribution patterns, which resulted in increased active sites. To verify the regulation mechanism of carbon dots on Ni(OH)2 for optimizing MOR activity, advanced techniques such as in-situ Raman, in-situ FTIR, operando EIS and EXAFS are used. The synergistic effect mechanism of α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2 for electrocatalytic oxidation of methanol into formate are supposed. This work will provide important guidance for constructing efficient CDs-based electrocatalyst and controllably regulating the intrinsic activity of metal sites by CDs.

Ethylenediamine (99 %) was bought from Alfa Aesar. Citric acid (≥99.5 %) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd. Graphite powder was purchased from Qingdao Huatai Lubrication Sealing Technology Co., Ltd. Propylene carbonate (PC, ≥99.9 %) and tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBABF4, ≥98.0 %) were acquired from Aladdin. Ammonia solution was supplied by Tianjin Kemyo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and ethanol was provided by Tianjin Beichen Founder Reagent Factory. Nickel wire (5 mm diameter) was purchased from Runde Metal Materials Company. All chemicals were used without further purification.

Graphene was produced by electrochemical cathodic exfoliation. Initially, an electrolyte solution was prepared by dissolving TBABF4 (1 mM) in 15 mL PC. Graphite powder (40 mg) served as the cathode, while a separate Pt foil was utilized as the anode. The exfoliation was proceeded under an applied potential of 30 V for 12 h. Subsequently, the resulting material was subjected to ultrasonic dispersion, followed by filtration with ethanol, and then dried to obtain intercalated graphene. Finally, the intercalated graphene was thermally treated at 600 °C for 1 h under nitrogen atmosphere to get fluffy graphene.

3.4 mL ethylenediamine, 40 mL NH3·H2O, 4 g citric acid and 40 mg graphene were added separately to the mixture of water and ethanol (1:1 ratio). The resulting mixture was then subjected to sonication. Then the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined container and put in a oven at 140 °C for 3 h, then heated up to 200 °C for another 3 h. After washing the product three times with anhydrous ethanol, and subsequently drying under vacuum for 6 h, 4CDs/G was yielded. Based on the varying amounts of added carbon dot precursors, 2CDs/G and 6CDs/G composites were also synthesized (1.7 mL ethylenediamine for 2CDs/G and 5.1 mL ethylenediamine for 6CDs/G).

The Ni-CDs/G composites were fabricated utilizing the electrochemical-solvothermal method. Initially, an electrolyte solution was prepared by dissolving TBABF4 (1 mM) in 15 mL PC. Platinum foil was employed as the cathode, while a polished nickel wire served as the anode. A high voltage of 30 V was applied for 6 h. Upon completion of the electrolysis, 40 mg of the previously prepared CDs/G and 25 mL of PC were added to the resulted electrolyte solution, getting a 40 mL mixture containing nickel ions, CDs/G, and the electrolyte solution. The mixture was put into a 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave after sonication and heated to 160 °C for 12 h. Ultimately, the samples designated as Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, and Ni-6CDs/G were achieved. The amount of carbon dots be controlled is regulated by the precursor (citric acid) of carbon dots.

The crystal structures were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), which were conducted by Ultima IV185 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα1 radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted on a Thermo Fisher Scientific instrument using an Al Kα X-ray source. The morphology of these materials was recorded using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-2100 microscope. The in-situ electrochemical Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (in-situ FTIR) were recorded using Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer in the wavenumber range of 700–4000 cm−1. The confocal Raman microscope (InVia, Renishaw) with a 50x objective were applied to record the operando Raman spectra, and the laser excitation wavelength is 532 nm. The spectra of Raman were recorded in a tailor-made Teflon cell under certain potentials controlled by a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy was performed by the RapidXAFS 2 M (Anhui Absorption Spectroscopy Analysis Instrument Co., Ltd.) via transmission (or fluorescence) mode at 20 kV and 20 mA, and the Si (551) spherically bent crystal analyzer which has a 500 mm curvature radius was applied for Ni.

All of the electrochemical measurements were carried out on the CHI 760E workstation using a classical three-electrode system. The catalyst is coated on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) serving as the working electrode, mercury/mercuric oxide electrode (Hg/HgO) is provided as the reference electrode and the graphite rod is supported as counter electrode. The GCE was firstly polished with alumina powder(20 ∼ 50 nm in diameter), followed by ultrasonic in a mixture of ethanol and water. 5 mg of catalyst and 30 μL of a 5 wt% Nafion solution were added in 1 mL of a mixed solvent (ethanol/water volume ratio of 1:3), and the mixture was sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous electrocatalytic ink. The working electrode was prepared by dropping 5 μL of the ink (containing 0.025 mg of catalyst) onto the treated GCE. All the tests were conducted in 1 M KOH containing 1 M methanol. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) were recorded within a potential window of 0 V–1.8 V (vs RHE) at a scan rate of 50 mV s −1 to calculate the current density, the Tafel slope and mass activity. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed from the frequency 100000 Hz to 0.01 Hz. To obtain the values of double-layer capacitance (Cdl), CV curves were recorded in the non-Faradaic region at scan rates of 20 mV s −1, 40 mV s −1, 60 mV s −1, 80 mV s −1, 100 mV s −1, 120 mV s −1, 160 mV s −1, and 200 mV s −1. The stability of the catalysts was analyzed by comparing polarization curves recorded before and after 2000 CV cycles. According to the Nernst equation, all electrode potentials were converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potentials: E (vs RHE) = E (vs Hg/HgO) + 0.098 V + 0.0591 pH.

Here, these Ni-CDs/G samples are synthesized elaborately through three processes (Fig. 1 ). In the electrochemical exfoliation process, graphene with high electronic conductivity is prepared by electrochemical intercalating TBA+ into graphite layers and the expanded graphite is then exfoliated into graphene by annealing process [24]. In the subsequent hydrothermal process, CDs is in-situ formed and deposited on graphene. Correspondingly, conductive 3D heterostructure of CDs/G is successfully synthesized. Noticeably, CDs as anchoring points can improve the anchoring and dispersion of Ni species. In the final process, the precursor of Ni species generated from anodic electrolysis are successfully loaded on CDs/G through the subsequent solvothermal process. To verify the influence of CDs on Ni, the concentration of CDs is regulated and a series of Ni-CDs/G samples (Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, Ni-6CDs/G) are prepared. This self-designed method could not only provide CDs/G supporter with high electronic conductivity, but also obtain amorphous and defective Ni species.

XRD spectra indicate that all the samples mainly display the phase of graphite (Fig. 2 a), inferred from the characteristic peaks (26.4° (002), 44.4° (101), 54.5° (004), PDF#41-1487) for graphite. Besides, it can be seen from amplified XRD spectra that the characteristic peak of Ni species is also found in Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, Ni-6CDs/G at about 52.1°, which is assigned to Ni(OH)2 ((102) PDF#14-0117). However, this characteristic peak of Ni species is very broad and low in intensity, suggesting that Ni(OH)2 species with low-crystallinity are formed on these samples. Noticeably, this peak position of Ni(OH)2 exhibits a negative shift in comparison with standard peak, this may be caused by the insertion of electrolyte and expanded interlayer spacing. Different from Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G, Ni/G doesn’t show the Ni species, probably due to its much lower crystallinity or relatively low loading amount of Ni species. ICP-OES results (Table S1) further verify that Ni has been successfully loaded on graphene. The loading amounts of Ni in Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, and Ni-6CDs/G are 17.9 %, 20.1 %, 22.5 % and 27.2 %, respectively. It is also observed that the loading amount of Ni increases with the enhancement of carbon dots. From CV curve (Fig. 2b), it can be seen the oxidation peaks of Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH are present on Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, Ni-6CDs/G from 1.25 V to 1.55 V. This suggests Ni(OH)2 species are generated in Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, and Ni-6CDs/G, agreeing with XRD results. The alterations in Ni coordination environment and local electronic structure has been tracked by Ni K-edge X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy [25,26]. From the XANES, the oxidation state of the metal in a statistical nature could be analyzed based on the white line intensity and edge position. Fig. 2c shows that the white line peak of Ni species in Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, Ni-6CDs/G is very close to that in NiO, which is positioned between Ni foil and Ni2O3, further verifying the formation of Ni(II). The Fourier transform of extended X-ray absorption fine structure (FT-EXAFS) spectra is relevant with the atom radial distribution near Ni atom. The FT-EXAFS spectra of Ni foil only display a single peak at 2.09 Å, which can be ascribed to the bonds of Ni-Ni (Fig. 2d). Different from Ni foil, NiO shows two peaks at around 1.63 Å and 2.55 Å, which is assigned to scattering path of Ni-O bonds and Ni-Ni bonds, respectively. Here the peak intensity of Ni-O bonds is much lower than that of Ni-Ni bonds. As for these as-prepared Ni-based samples (Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, Ni-6CDs/G), they all show two peaks of Ni-O bonds and Ni-Ni bonds. However, it’s found the Ni-O bond distance in Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G, Ni-6CDs/G is lower than that of NiO, but their Ni-Ni bond distances are higher than that of NiO. Moreover, in NiO, the intensity of Ni-O peak is much lower than that of Ni-Ni peak. While for Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G, the intensity of Ni-O peak is much higher than that of Ni-Ni peak. This suggests Ni local electronic structure and coordination environment in Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G are different from Ni foil and NiO, due to their formation phase of Ni(OH)2. Different crystal structures of Ni(OH)2 are regarded as the layered accumulation of NiO2. Interbedded particles and interlayer spacing in different Ni(OH)2 structures are quite different. Moreover, from the amplifying figure (Fig. 2e), it is obvious that the bond distances of Ni-O bond and Ni-Ni bond in Ni-4CDs/G are different from that in Ni-2CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G, indicating the variant Ni coordination environment in Ni-4CDs/G. This is also concluded that the Ni coordination environment in Ni-CDs/G can be regulated by carbon dots. The wavelet transform of EXAFS spectra (WT-EXAFS) is able to reveal the overlapped details in R-space by presenting radial distance resolution and k-space resolution. Consistent with the results of FT-EXAFS spectra, the scattering path signals of Ni-O bonds and Ni-Ni bonds are found in Ni-4CDs/G (Fig. 2f). The signal intensity of Ni-O bonds is higher than that of Ni-Ni bonds. The scattering path signal of Ni-O bond is closely next to that of Ni-Ni bond.

As shown in electrocatalytic MOR, NiOOH is considered as an electrocatalytically active species which is evolved from Ni(OH)2. The MOR onset potentials are almost perfectly coincide with the potential for Ni(II)/Ni(III) electro-oxidation, suggesting that the MOR occurs immediately after the active NiOOH species are electrochemically generated on the surface. The bending and stretching vibrations of Ni3+-O in NiOOH is monitored by In-situ Raman spectroscopy [27–29]. As can be seen from the Raman spectra (Fig. 3 a–d), Ni3+-O bending (δ(Ni-O)) and stretching (ν(Ni-O)) of NiOOH is observed at 474 and 551 cm−1, respectively. It means the NiOOH species are actual active sites and the electrocatalytic conversion of methanol is carried out on NiOOH species. That’s to say, in this MOR mechanism, it follow the indirect methanol oxidation mechanism and the conversion of Ni(OH)2/NiOOH is the rate-determining step. Generally, NiOOH has two crystals: β phase and γ phase. The δ(Ni-O)-to-ν(Ni-O) ratio (labeled to Iδ/ν) in γ-NiOOH is higher because of its more disordered looser structure. Therefore, higher Iδ/ν means more γ-NiOOH is formed rather than β-NiOOH. As shown in Fig. 3a–d, the average Iδ/ν of Ni-4CDs/G is 2.58, much higher than Ni/G (2.08), Ni-2CDs/G (2.00), and Ni-6CDs/G (1.99). This suggests more γ-NiOOH species are generated in Ni-4CDs/G than that in other samples. Moreover, such a higher Iδ/ν suggests methanol adsorption is positively affected by the local structure around Ni-O. From above, it’s concluded that more production of γ-NiOOH is benefitting for MOR. Here the γ‑NiOOH is evolved from α-Ni(OH)2 while β‑NiOOH is generated from β‑Ni(OH)2. It’s concluded that formation of more α-Ni(OH)2 is beneficial for adsorption of methanol, which contributes to superior MOR performance.

To verify the oxidation state of Ni, XPS technology is applied. Fig. 4 a shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p. The two obvious peaks at about 856.4 and 874.1 eV with two shakeup satellites are assigned to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni(OH)2 phase. This result is coincident with the analysis of XRD and CV results. From the Fig. 4a, it’s seen that both Ni-2CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G show a positive shift in Ni 2p XPS in comparison with the best Ni-4CDs/G. This is caused by phase-ratio variation of α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2, which is derived from carbon dots. This is proved by the in-situ Raman spectra that α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2 ratio in Ni-4CDs/G is higher than that in Ni-2CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G. Here the actual ratio of Ni and carbon dots can be determined by XPS technique. The N is doped in carbon dots and amount of N could reflect the carbon dots. Thus, the actual ratio of Ni and carbon dots could be replaced with ratio of Ni and N in carbon dots. As shown in Fig. S1, both N peak and Ni peak appear in XPS survey spectra of Ni-4CDs/G. From the calculation results (Table S2) based on XPS, it’s seen that molar ratios of N/Ni for Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G are 0.076, 0.13 and 0.17 respectively. It’s concluded that actual ratio of carbon dots/Ni for these Ni-CDs/G samples increases with the enhanced amount of the precursor (citric acid). As displayed in Fig. 4b, the work function (Eϕ) for Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G nanosheets are calculated to be 6.44 eV, 6.18 eV, 5.86 eV and 5.85 eV respectively through the equation Eϕ = hν − Eonset [7,30,31]. In addition, the valence band maximum (EV) can be derived from the UPS spectrum by linearly extrapolating the leading edge to the baseline. The derived EV are located 3.62 eV, 3.81 eV, 4.75 eV and 4.41 eV below the EF of Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G nanosheets, respectively. Fig. 4c illustrates the schematic energy band alignment diagrams obtained by the UPS results. Compared with other Ni-based samples, the higher EF and lower Eϕ of Ni-4CDs/G suggest better electric conductivity. Besides, the d states are mainly resulted from the valence electrons near the EF, while the EV of Ni-4CDs/G is located further away from EF, indicating the d band center of Ni(OH)2 is downshifted from EF via regulation of carbon dots [32]. Overall, the d band center of Ni(OH)2 in Ni-CDs/G can be adjusted by carbon dots (Fig. 4d), which finally determines the MOR performance of Ni-CDs/G.

Raman spectra are used to evaluate the degree of defect and detect the metal–oxygen species [33,34]. Two peaks of D peak (about 1350 cm−1) and G peak (about 1580 cm−1) are showed up in Fig. 5 a. The intrinsic defects of electrocatalysts can be reflected from the ID/IG ratio. As a result, defect degree of these samples enhances with the increased loading amount of carbon dots. Fig. 5b shows that the loading amount of Ni increases with enhanced loading amount of carbon dots, suggesting that carbon dots play an important role in anchoring Ni species. HRTEM images show that Ni-2CDs/G (Fig. 5c), Ni-4CDs/G (Fig. 5d), Ni-6CDs/G (Fig. 5e) and Ni/G (Fig. S2) mainly present the Ni species of Ni(OH)2 ((102) d = 0.175 nm PDF#14–0117), consistent with XRD results. In addition, the influence of carbon dots on the distribution of Ni species is also revealed (Fig. 5f). With low loading amount of carbon dots, such as Ni/G and Ni-2CDs/G, Ni species are obviously agglomerated. With the increased loading amount of carbon dots, the dispersion of Ni species is greatly improved because of strong interaction between carbon dots and Ni species. But with the loading amount of carbon dots increased further, carbon dots are agglomerated obviously. Thus, it can be concluded that the amount of carbon dots could be used to disperse Ni species and regulate their distribution configuration. HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 5g) of Ni-4CDs/G and the corresponding EDS maps indicate the homogeneous spatial distributions of nickel (Fig. 5g1), carbon (Fig. 5g2), oxygen (Fig. 5g3) and nitrogen (Fig. 5g4). This result further reveals that Ni species highly disperse on graphene substrate. Just as the EDS mapping shows, the existence of N element is proved in the catalysts. Specifically speaking, the N is doped in carbon dots and originate from the raw material (NH3·H2O). Here the existence of N element not only verifies the successful coupling of carbon dots with graphene, but also could alter the electronic structure of adjacent carbon in carbon dots for further affecting Ni species.

To investigate MOR activity of these catalysts, cyclic voltammetry (CV) is firstly applied. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, specific activities of these as-prepared catalysts are displayed. It’s seen the pure graphene didn’t show MOR activity. However, with introduction of carbon dots, the obtained 4CDs/G exhibits much higher MOR activity than pure graphene, indicating the carbon dots also contributes to MOR activity. That’s because abundant oxygenated groups and defects in carbon dots provide electrocatalytic sites for MOR. Among these samples, Ni-4CDs/G displays the best specific activity with an area peak current of 299.2 mA cm−2, much higher than Ni/G (99.6 mA cm−2), Ni-2CDs/G (225.8 mA cm−2), and Ni-6CDs/G (282.5 mA cm−2). Moreover, the mass activities (MA) of these samples are also calculated (Fig. 6c and d). The results of mass activities are consistent with the specific activities results, Ni-4CDs/G also exhibits the highest mass activity which is 3756.5 mA mg−1. It’s seen our obtained Ni-CDs/G exhibits competitive MOR activity in comparison with recent works (Table S3) [1–3,35–41]. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 6e indicate that the charge transfer resistance of Ni-4CDs/G is lower than that of Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G. The charge transfer resistance values (Rct) are obtained according to the Randles circuit. As seen from Fig. 6e, the Rct value for Ni-4CDs/G is 58 Ω, lower than that of other samples. The high electron transfer rates might contribute to the enhanced MOR activity of Ni-4CDs/G catalyst. The electrochemical active surfaces (ECSAs) are derived based on Cdl (Fig. 6f), which is calculated according to non-faradaic CV results (Fig. S3). Ni-4CDs/G exhibits much higher ECSA than Ni/G, Ni-2CDs/G, and Ni-6CDs/G. The higher ECSA and more active sites of Ni-4CDs/G are originated from the highly dispersed Ni species regulated by carbon dots. The turnover frequency (TOF) results further confirmed this result. The TOF value in Fig. 6g shows that Ni-4CDs/G has a highest TOF of 0.31 s−1 at 1.38 V (vs. RHE), much higher than that of Ni/G (0.13 s−1), Ni-2CDs/G (0.28 s−1), and Ni-6CDs/G (0.25 s−1). The durability of Ni-4CDs/G is also tested. At 50 mA cm−2, its activity could remain without attenuation for 1440 min (24 h) (Fig. 6h). To improve the overall water electrolysis efficiency, MOR is used to replace OER and coupled with HER (Fig. 6i). The anode is coated with Ni-4CDs/G and the cathode is loaded with Pt/C. For comparison, an OER//HER cell is also assembled with RuO2 as the anode in 1 M KOH. The test result shows that the initial overpotential for methanol electrolysis is 140 mV lower than that of water electrolysis, further verifying the superior MOR activity over Ni-4CDs/G.

To investigate the electrochemical mechanism, operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is measured (Fig. 7 a–d) [2,42–44]. The peaks showed up in high frequency region and low frequency region can be ascribed to electrode oxidation and electrooxidation reaction occurred on surface oxidized species respectively. It’s considered that OER occurs at the formed oxidization layer due to electrooxidation of the electrocatalyst. Different from G (Fig. S4) and CDs/G (Fig. S5), operando EIS results indicate these obtained Ni-based samples show obvious oxidation peaks towards MOR in the region between low frequency and high frequency, indicating that Ni species are real active centers. Moreover, an obvious oxidation peak of Ni species is observed in Ni/G (Fig. S6), but it doesn’t appear in Ni-2CDs/G (Fig. 7a), Ni-4CDs/G (Fig. 7b) and Ni-6CDs/G (Fig. 7c). This indicates a higher MOR activity exists in Ni-2CDs/G, Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G, because the produced NiOOH from Ni(OH)2 can be quickly consumed by reactants and intermediates. As for Ni-4CDs/G, compared with operando EIS results of MOR, its OER results are different (Fig. 7d). Obvious OER peak is present in the low frequency region, while the oxidation peak of catalyst appears in the high frequency region.

To detect the intermediate and explain the mechanism of MOR on these as-prepared samples, in-situ FTIR spectroscopy is employed [8,45,46]. As shown in Fig. 8 a–d, the IR adsorption peak detected at 1620–1630 cm−1 is assigned to the OH bending mode, which stands for the concentration variation of OH– near the electrode. The intensity of these absorbed OH– peaks over Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G is higher than that of Ni-2CDs/G and Ni/G, indicating more OH* are absorbed on Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G. The peak at about 1040 cm−1 can be ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-OH band of methanol. It’s concluded that more methanol molecules are absorbed on Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G, than that over Ni-2CDs/G, consistent with methanol-TPD results. The relatively strong adsorption of OH− and CH3OH on Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G, portending excellent MOR activity. In these three Ni-based samples, three characteristic bands at 1340–1345 cm−1, 1370–1375 cm−1 and 1554–1559 cm−1 can also be detected, these bands are ascribed to three different characteristic groups of HCOOH. This observation suggests that the main MOR product is HCOOH over these electrocatalysts. The HCOOH formation follows the path of CH3OH → *CH3OH → * CH3O → *CH2O → *CHO → *HCOOH → HCOOH. The higher peak intensity of HCOOH groups in Ni-4CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G indicate more HCOOH product is generated, agreeing well with electrochemical test results. Methanol-TPD is applied to judge the adsorption properties of all as-prepared samples towards methanol (Fig. 8e) [47]. It can be observed that no adsorption peak of methanol showed up for 4CDs/G and G, while all Ni-based samples show an obvious adsorption peak of methanol in the range of 300 °C-350 °C. This result indicates Ni sites are the real adsorption sites for methanol. Ni-4CDs/G shows a higher desorption temperature (325 °C) for methanol molecules than Ni-2CDs/G (318 °C) and Ni-6CDs/G (319 °C), implying stronger methanol adsorption on Ni-4CDs/G. It’s concluded that the high ratio of α-Ni(OH)2/β‑Ni(OH)2 promotes the CH3OH adsorption and activation, which lead to improved MOR activity. Moreover, compared with other Ni-based materials, more adsorption amount of methanol is observed in Ni-4CDs/G. This also indicates much higher electrocatalytic activity towards MOR. Based on Arrhenius equation and CVs recorded at different temperatures, the E a value is derived (Fig. S7–S9) [48,49]. The calculated E a value of the Ni-4CDs/G catalyst is 12.04 kJ/mol (Fig. 8f), much lower than that of other Ni-based catalysts. The lowest E a value Ni-4CDs/G imply that this catalyst displays the highest intrinsic activity for MOR.

Defective α-Ni(OH)2 with high electric conductivity is beneficial for reactant adsorption and electron transfer. While β‑NiOOH electrooxidized from small interlayer spacing β‑Ni(OH)2 is more reactive for methanol oxidation than γ‑NiOOH derived from α-Ni(OH)2. A catalytic reaction goes through the process of adsorption-reaction-desorption, adsorption as the initial step directly affects the subsequent series process. Therefore, the synergistic catalytic activity of α-Ni (OH)2 and β-Ni (OH)2 for methanol is higher than that of α-Ni(OH)2 and β-Ni(OH)2 alone. Just as shown in Fig. 9 a, our experimental and characterization results show that a relatively higher ratio of α-Ni(OH)2/β-NiOOH in Ni-4CDs/G exhibits contributes to higher intrinsic activity towards methanol conversion in comparison with Ni-2CDs/G and Ni-6CDs/G as inferred from compared values of MA, TOF and Ea. This is because more α-Ni(OH)2 sites promote the adsorption of methanol, and the high concentration of methanol around α-Ni(OH)2 migrates to the adjacent β-Ni (OH)2 sites where methanol is reacted into formic acid (Fig. 9b), leading to highly efficient electrocatalytic activity of methanol oxidation. Here, the ratio of α-Ni(OH)2/β-Ni(OH)2 is successfully regulated by the CDs. CDs have rich C-O/C=O/COOH functional groups, which can interact with Ni-O/Ni-O in Ni (OH)2, this interaction causes highly dispersed CDs&Ni(OH)2 on graphene and relatively higher ratio of α-Ni(OH)2/β-Ni(OH)2 which shows excellent electrocatalytic performance for methanol oxidation.

In summary, Ni-CDs/G samples are synthesized successfully through a facile electric field-assisted synthesis method. Among these as-prepared samples, Ni-4CDs/G shows the best MOR activity with specific activity of 299.2 mA cm−2 and mass activity of 3756.5 mA mg−1 at peak current. Moreover, highest TOF value of 0.31 s−1 at 1.38 V and lowest E a value of 12.04 kJ/mol also could be observed. In water splitting test, lower initial overpotential of 140 mV is found in methanol electrolysis than that in water electrolysis. The promotion of CDs to the MOR activity of Ni-4CDs/G is attributed to two aspects. Firstly, moderate amount of carbon dots facilitates the formation of highly dispersed CDs&Ni(OH)2, which contributed to more reactive sites and ECSAs for MOR. Secondly, the CDs greatly promotes the intrinsic activity of Ni(OH)2 in Ni-4CDs/G towards MOR by regulating the Ni local electronic structure and coordination environment. This accelerates the formation of more α-Ni(OH)2 and d-band center downshift of Ni(OH)2 from the EF level, which is beneficial for adsorption of methanol and its conversion into formate via the path of CH3OH → *CH3OH → * CH3O → *CH2O → *CHO → *HCOOH → HCOOH.
